questions.question:
Consider an instruction pipeline with four stages (S1, S2, S3 and S4) each with combinational circuit only. The pipeline registers are required between each stage and at the end of the last stage. Delays for the stages and for the pipeline registers are as given in the figure.

Stage delays: S1 = 5 ns, S2 = 6 ns, S3 = 11 ns, S4 = 8 ns
Pipeline register delay: 1 ns each (5 registers total, one after each stage)

What is the approximate speed up of the pipeline in steady state under ideal conditions when compared to the corresponding non-pipeline implementation?

A. 4.0
B. 2.5
C. 1.1
D. 3.0
questions.to_find: Approximate speedup of 4-stage pipeline in steady state compared to non-pipelined execution

understand.plain: We have a 4-stage pipeline where each stage has a combinational delay and there are pipeline registers (with 1 ns delay each) between stages and after the last stage. In steady state, the clock cycle time equals the maximum stage delay plus pipeline register delay. Speedup = non-pipelined time / pipelined clock cycle time.

keywords.explain: The clock period must accommodate the slowest stage plus the pipeline register delay. It equals max(stage delays) + register delay.
keywords.example: If max stage = 11 ns and register = 1 ns, clock cycle = 12 ns.

keywords.explain: Ratio of non-pipelined execution time to pipelined execution time for a single instruction (or in steady state). Non-pipelined time = sum of all stage delays.
keywords.example: Non-pipelined: 5+6+11+8 = 30 ns; pipelined clock: 12 ns; speedup = 30/12 = 2.5.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='70' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='45' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S1</text><text x='45' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>5 ns</text><rect x='100' y='40' width='20' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R</text><rect x='140' y='30' width='70' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='175' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S2</text><text x='175' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>6 ns</text><rect x='230' y='40' width='20' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R</text><rect x='270' y='30' width='70' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='305' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S3</text><text x='305' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>11 ns</text><rect x='360' y='40' width='20' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='370' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R</text><rect x='400' y='30' width='70' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='435' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S4</text><text x='435' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>8 ns</text><rect x='490' y='40' width='20' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='500' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R</text><text x='260' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>R = pipeline register (1 ns each)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four pipeline stages S1(5ns), S2(6ns), S3(11ns), S4(8ns) connected by pipeline registers R(1ns each). S3 is the bottleneck stage.

given.aim: Calculate speedup of 4-stage pipeline over non-pipelined execution

terms.meaning: S1=5ns, S2=6ns, S3=11ns, S4=8ns — combinational delays for each pipeline stage
terms.example: S3 takes 11 ns to process its portion of an instruction
terms.connects: Sum gives non-pipelined time; max gives pipeline bottleneck

terms.meaning: Each pipeline register adds 1 ns overhead to the clock cycle
terms.example: Register between S1 and S2 adds 1 ns
terms.connects: Added to the maximum stage delay to determine clock cycle time

terms.meaning: Total time for one instruction without pipelining = sum of all stage delays (no registers needed)

given.plan: 1) Compute non-pipelined time = sum of all stage delays. 2) Compute pipelined clock cycle = max(stage delays) + register delay. 3) Speedup = non-pipelined time / pipelined clock cycle.

steps.title: Non-pipelined execution time
steps.note: In non-pipelined mode, all stages execute sequentially for each instruction; pipeline registers are not needed.

steps.title: Pipelined clock cycle time
steps.formula_raw: T_clock = max(S1, S2, S3, S4) + T_register
steps.note: S3 is the bottleneck. The pipeline register delay (1 ns) is added because registers are clocked at every cycle.

steps.title: Speedup calculation
steps.note: This is the speedup for a single instruction in steady state. The answer is approximately 2.5.

questions.formula_note: Pipeline speedup = (sum of all stage delays) / (max stage delay + pipeline register delay). The bottleneck stage determines the clock cycle time.

questions.question:
Register renaming is done in pipelined processors:

A. as an alternative to register allocation at compile time
B. for efficient access to function parameters and local variables
C. to handle certain kinds of hazards
D. as part of address translation
questions.answer: C. to handle certain kinds of hazards
questions.to_find: The primary purpose of register renaming in pipelined processors

understand.plain: Register renaming is a hardware technique used in out-of-order and pipelined processors. It eliminates false data dependencies (WAR and WAW hazards) that arise when multiple instructions use the same architectural register name but are logically independent. By mapping architectural registers to a larger set of physical registers, the processor can execute instructions that would otherwise stall.

keywords.explain: A hardware mechanism that maps architectural (logical) register names to physical registers, removing false dependencies between instructions.
keywords.example: If I1 writes R1 and I2 also writes R1 (WAW), renaming maps them to different physical registers P1 and P2 so both can proceed.

keywords.explain: Write-After-Read (WAR) and Write-After-Write (WAW) are false data hazards caused by reuse of register names, not true data dependencies. Register renaming eliminates these.
keywords.example: WAW: R1 = R2+R3 followed by R1 = R4+R5 — both write R1. With renaming, each gets a distinct physical register.

understand.visual_alt: Register renaming maps architectural register R1 to physical registers P5 and P7 for two different instructions, eliminating WAW/WAR hazards.

given.aim: Identify the reason register renaming is used in pipelined processors

terms.meaning: The fixed set of registers visible to the programmer (e.g., R0-R31 in RISC)
terms.example: R1, R2, R3 in a 32-register ISA
terms.connects: Reuse of these names causes false hazards in pipelining

terms.meaning: A larger internal pool of registers that the hardware uses when renaming
terms.example: A processor may have 128 physical registers mapped to 32 architectural registers
terms.connects: Extra physical registers eliminate name-based conflicts

terms.meaning: A condition that prevents the next instruction in the pipeline from executing in the next clock cycle
terms.example: WAW: two instructions both write the same register
terms.connects: Register renaming removes WAR and WAW hazards specifically

given.plan: Eliminate options by reasoning: (A) compile-time register allocation is a compiler job, not the same as hardware renaming; (B) stack frames handle local variables; (D) address translation is TLB/MMU. Register renaming is specifically for hazard elimination.

steps.title: Analyze each option
steps.apply:
A: Register allocation at compile time assigns variables to registers — a compiler task, unrelated to run-time hardware renaming.
B: Function parameters and local variables are accessed via stack (frame pointer) — unrelated to register renaming.
C: Register renaming eliminates WAR (Write-After-Read) and WAW (Write-After-Write) hazards, which are false hazards caused by register name reuse.
D: Address translation is performed by the MMU/TLB — completely unrelated to register renaming.
steps.note: WAR and WAW are also called anti-dependencies and output dependencies. Register renaming removes them so out-of-order execution can proceed.

steps.title: Confirm the correct answer
steps.apply: Register renaming is a dynamic hardware technique to handle false data hazards (WAR, WAW). It does NOT handle structural hazards or RAW (true data) hazards — those require other mechanisms like forwarding or stalling.
steps.note: This is a well-known GATE concept: register renaming handles false hazards specifically.

solution.result: Option C: Register renaming is done to handle certain kinds of hazards (specifically WAR and WAW false data hazards).

questions.formula_note: Register renaming eliminates WAR (anti-dependency) and WAW (output dependency) hazards by mapping architectural register names to distinct physical registers dynamically.

questions.question:
Consider an instruction pipeline with five stages without any branch prediction: Fetch Instruction (FI), Decode Instruction (DI), Fetch Operand (FO), Execute Instruction (EI) and Write Operand (WO). The stage delays for FI, DI, FO, EI and WO are 5 ns, 7 ns, 10 ns, 8 ns, 6 ns, respectively. There are intermediate storage buffers after each stage and the delay of each buffer is 1 ns. A program consisting of 12 instructions I1, I2, I3, ..., I12 is executed on this pipelined processor. Instruction I5 is the only branch instruction and its branch target is I9. If the branch is taken during the execution of this program, the time (in ns) needed to complete the program is

A. 132
B. 165
C. 176
D. 328
questions.to_find: Total time (in ns) to complete 12 instructions when I5 is a taken branch to I9

understand.plain: A 5-stage pipeline executes 12 instructions. Instruction I5 is a taken branch to I9. The branch is resolved during EI (stage 4). When I5 is in EI, the instructions I6 (in FO) and I7 (in DI) behind it are flushed. The slot freed by I8 in FI (the same cycle I5 enters EI) is used to fetch I9 instead. This gives a branch penalty of 2 cycles (I6 and I7 are wasted). Total execution = 9 valid instructions + 4 pipeline drain + 2 penalty cycles = 15 cycles.

keywords.explain: When a branch is taken and resolved at EI (stage 4), instructions that entered stages FO and DI after the branch instruction must be flushed. The FI slot in the same cycle EI executes can be reassigned to the branch target.
keywords.example: I5 in EI at cycle 8: I6 is in FO and I7 is in DI — both are flushed (2 penalty cycles). I9 fills FI at cycle 8.

keywords.explain: The pipeline clock period = max(stage delays) + buffer delay = 10 + 1 = 11 ns
keywords.example: FO stage = 10 ns is the bottleneck; add 1 ns buffer = 11 ns per clock cycle.

understand.visual_alt: 5-stage pipeline timeline: I5 in EI at cycle 8. I6 (FO) and I7 (DI) are flushed — 2 penalty cycles. I9 starts FI at cycle 8. I12 completes WO at cycle 15. Total = 15 * 11 = 165 ns.

given.aim: Compute total execution time in ns for 12 instructions with one taken branch (I5 -> I9)

terms.meaning: FI=5ns, DI=7ns, FO=10ns, EI=8ns, WO=6ns — individual stage processing times
terms.example: FO (Fetch Operand) at 10 ns is the bottleneck stage
terms.connects: Clock cycle time = max(5,7,10,8,6) + 1 buffer = 11 ns

terms.meaning: The branch outcome is known when I5 reaches EI. At that clock cycle, I6 is in FO and I7 is in DI — these two must be flushed. I8 has not yet started FI (its FI slot is replaced by I9).
terms.example: Cycle 8: I5 in EI, I6 in FO (flushed), I7 in DI (flushed), I9 in FI (new fetch).
terms.connects: Branch penalty = 2 wasted instruction slots (I6 and I7)

terms.meaning: Valid instructions = I1..I5 + I9..I12 = 9 instructions. Wasted slots = 2 (I6, I7). Total pipeline cycles = 9 + 4 (drain) + 2 (penalty) = 15.
terms.example: Without branch: 12 + 4 = 16 cycles. With branch (2 penalty): 9 + 4 + 2 = 15 cycles.

given.plan: 1) Find clock cycle = 11 ns. 2) Build pipeline timing diagram. 3) Count total clock cycles until last instruction (I12) completes WO. 4) Multiply by clock cycle time.

steps.title: Determine clock cycle time
steps.formula_raw: T_clock = max(FI, DI, FO, EI, WO) + T_buffer
steps.note: FO stage (10 ns) is the bottleneck. Buffer (register) delay of 1 ns is added to every stage.

steps.title: Pipeline timing diagram for I1 through I5
steps.apply:
I1: FI=1, DI=2, FO=3, EI=4, WO=5
I2: FI=2, DI=3, FO=4, EI=5, WO=6
I3: FI=3, DI=4, FO=5, EI=6, WO=7
I4: FI=4, DI=5, FO=6, EI=7, WO=8
I5: FI=5, DI=6, FO=7, EI=8, WO=9

Branch (I5) is in EI at cycle 8. At this moment:
- I6 is in FO (cycle 8) -- FLUSHED
- I7 is in DI (cycle 8) -- FLUSHED
- I8 would go to FI (cycle 8), but instead I9 is fetched at cycle 8.
steps.note: The branch is resolved at the beginning of (or during) EI. The FI stage in the same cycle can be redirected to the correct target I9. So only instructions already past FI (i.e., in DI and FO) are wasted — 2 penalty cycles.

steps.title: Pipeline timing diagram for I9 through I12
steps.apply:
I9:  FI=8,  DI=9,  FO=10, EI=11, WO=12
I10: FI=9,  DI=10, FO=11, EI=12, WO=13
I11: FI=10, DI=11, FO=12, EI=13, WO=14
I12: FI=11, DI=12, FO=13, EI=14, WO=15

I12 completes WO at cycle 15.
steps.note: I9 starts FI in cycle 8 (same cycle I5 is in EI, because the FI slot is redirected). The pipeline continues smoothly for I9-I12.

steps.title: Compute total execution time
steps.apply:
Total cycles = 15 (last cycle when I12 completes WO)
T_total = 15 * 11 = 165 ns
steps.note: Branch penalty = 2 cycles (I6, I7 flushed). Compared to no-branch case: 9 valid instr + 4 drain + 2 penalty = 15 cycles.

questions.formula_note: Pipeline execution time = total_clock_cycles * clock_cycle_time. Branch penalty = number of instructions flushed = (branch_resolution_stage - 1) - 1 = stages already past FI when branch is in EI. Here, FO and DI stages have I6, I7 = 2 penalty cycles. I9 replaces I8 in FI at the same cycle EI executes.

questions.question: Consider a 6-stage instruction pipeline, where all stages are perfectly balanced. Assume that there is no cycle-time overhead of pipelining. When an application is executing on this 6-stage pipeline, the speedup achieved with respect to non-pipelined execution if 25% of the instructions incur 2 pipeline stall cycles is ___.
questions.to_find: Speedup of 6-stage pipeline when 25% of instructions cause 2 stall cycles each

understand.plain: A perfectly balanced 6-stage pipeline means every stage takes the same time, so the clock cycle time equals the non-pipelined time divided by 6. Without stalls, speedup = 6. But 25% of instructions each cause 2 extra stall cycles, which reduces the effective speedup. We calculate the average cycles per instruction and derive speedup.

keywords.meaning: Extra idle cycles inserted into the pipeline when a hazard is detected, delaying the pipeline by 1 or more cycles per stall event.
keywords.example: A load-use hazard causes 1 stall cycle; here 25% of instructions cause 2 stall cycles.

keywords.meaning: Speedup = ideal CPI without pipeline / actual CPI with pipeline. For a balanced n-stage pipeline, speedup = n / (1 + stall cycles per instruction).
keywords.example: With 0 stalls: speedup = 6. With average 0.5 stall cycles/instruction: speedup = 6/1.5 = 4.

understand.visual_alt: 6-stage balanced pipeline: 75% of instructions complete in 1 cycle, 25% take 1+2=3 cycles. Average CPI = 0.75*1 + 0.25*3 = 1.5. Speedup = 6/1.5 = 4.

given.aim: Find speedup of 6-stage pipeline with 25% instructions causing 2 stall cycles

terms.example: Each stage takes T/6 time where T is non-pipelined instruction time

terms.meaning: 25% (0.25) of all instructions incur 2 extra stall cycles each
terms.example: Out of 100 instructions, 25 cause 2 stall cycles = 50 extra stall cycles total

terms.meaning: Average cycles per instruction = 1 (base) + average stall cycles = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5
terms.example: CPI = 1 for non-stalling instructions, CPI = 3 for stalling instructions. Average = 0.75*1 + 0.25*3 = 1.5

given.plan: 1) Compute average stall cycles per instruction = fraction * stalls = 0.25 * 2 = 0.5. 2) Average CPI = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5. 3) Speedup = n / CPI = 6 / 1.5 = 4. But since the pipeline is perfectly balanced and no cycle-time overhead, speedup = non-pipelined time / pipelined time = 6T / (1.5 * T_clock). With T_clock = T/6, pipelined time = 1.5 * T/6 = T/4. Speedup = T / (T/4) = 4.

steps.title: Average stall cycles per instruction
steps.note: 25% of instructions each add 2 stall cycles; the remaining 75% add 0 stall cycles.

steps.title: Average CPI of pipelined processor
steps.note: The ideal CPI of any pipeline is 1 (one instruction completes per cycle in steady state). Stalls add fractional cycles on average.

steps.title: Speedup calculation
steps.apply:
For a non-pipelined processor, CPI = n = 6 (all 6 stages run sequentially).
Speedup = 6 / 1.5 = 4.0
steps.note: Since the pipeline is perfectly balanced (no cycle-time overhead), the non-pipelined execution takes exactly n times longer than one ideal pipeline cycle. Speedup = n / (1 + avg_stall) = 6/1.5 = 4.

steps.title: Verify using instruction count approach
steps.apply:
For N instructions:
Non-pipeline time = N * 6 * T (executes all 6 stages sequentially per instruction)
Pipeline stall cycles = 0.25 * N * 2 = 0.5N extra cycles
Pipeline time (large N) = (N + 0.5N) * T = 1.5N * T
Speedup = (6N * T) / (1.5N * T) = 6 / 1.5 = 4.0
steps.note: Both methods (CPI-based and time-based) give the same speedup of 4.0. The perfectly balanced pipeline assumption means each stage takes the same time T, so non-pipelined = 6T and pipelined clock = T.

questions.formula_note: Pipeline speedup with stalls = n / (1 + average stall cycles per instruction), where average stall = (fraction of stalling instructions) * (stalls per stalling instruction). For n=6, 25% instructions with 2 stalls: speedup = 6/1.5 = 4.
